name: Molecular Biology — Sorting
description: Test your knowledge of the **correct order of molecular biology processes** — from DNA replication and transcription to translation and beyond.

questions.text: Sort the stages of the **cell cycle** in the correct order:

choices.text: G1 phase — cell growth and preparation
S phase — DNA synthesis/replication
G2 phase — growth and preparation for division
M phase / Mitosis — nuclear division
Cytokinesis — cytoplasm division

questions.explanation:
The cell cycle has four main phases followed by cytokinesis:

1. **G1 (Gap 1)** — the cell grows in size, synthesises proteins, and prepares for DNA replication; checkpoint ensures conditions are favourable
2. **S (Synthesis)** — DNA replication occurs; each chromosome is duplicated to form two sister chromatids
3. **G2 (Gap 2)** — further growth and protein synthesis; the cell checks for DNA replication errors before entering mitosis
4. **M phase (Mitosis)** — the nucleus divides; chromosomes are separated into two identical nuclei (prophase → metaphase → anaphase → telophase)
5. **Cytokinesis** — the cytoplasm divides, producing two genetically identical daughter cells

G1, S, and G2 together form **interphase**, which occupies ~90% of the cell cycle.

questions.text: Sort the phases of **mitosis** in the correct order:

choices.text: Prophase — chromosomes condense, spindle forms
Metaphase — chromosomes align at the cell equator
Anaphase — sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles
Telophase — nuclear envelopes reform around each set of chromosomes

questions.explanation:
Mitosis is divided into four sequential phases:

1. **Prophase** — chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes; the mitotic spindle begins to form from centrosomes
2. **Metaphase** — chromosomes are fully condensed and align along the metaphase plate (cell equator); spindle fibres attach to kinetochores
3. **Anaphase** — sister chromatids are separated and pulled to opposite poles of the cell by shortening spindle fibres
4. **Telophase** — chromosomes arrive at the poles and begin to decondense; nuclear envelopes reform around each set, producing two nuclei

Mnemonic: **PMAT** — **P**ro → **M**eta → **A**na → **T**elo

questions.text: Sort the steps of **DNA replication** in the correct order:

choices.text: Helicase unwinds and separates the double helix at the origin of replication
Primase synthesizes a short RNA primer complementary to the template strand
DNA polymerase extends the new strand by adding deoxyribonucleotides (5'→3')
DNA ligase joins the Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand
Exonuclease removes RNA primers and DNA polymerase fills the gaps

questions.explanation:
DNA replication is semi-conservative and proceeds as follows:

1. **Helicase** — breaks hydrogen bonds and unwinds the double helix at the origin of replication, creating a replication fork
2. **Primase** — synthesises a short RNA primer (~10 nucleotides) to provide a free 3'-OH group for DNA polymerase
3. **DNA polymerase III** — extends the new strand by adding complementary deoxyribonucleotides in the 5'→3' direction (leading strand continuously; lagging strand in Okazaki fragments)
4. **DNA ligase** — seals the nicks between Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand, forming a continuous strand
5. **Exonuclease / DNA polymerase I** — removes the RNA primers and fills the resulting gaps with DNA

The result is two identical double-stranded DNA molecules, each with one original and one new strand.

questions.text: Sort the steps of **transcription in eukaryotes** in the correct order:

choices.text: Transcription factors bind to the promoter and recruit RNA polymerase II
RNA polymerase unwinds the DNA double helix at the transcription start site
RNA polymerase synthesizes the pre-mRNA strand (5'→3')
The 5' cap and poly-A tail are added to the pre-mRNA
Introns are removed by the spliceosome and exons are joined (splicing)

questions.explanation:
Eukaryotic transcription and pre-mRNA processing:

1. **Initiation** — general transcription factors assemble at the promoter (TATA box) and recruit RNA polymerase II to form the pre-initiation complex
2. **Unwinding** — RNA polymerase opens the DNA double helix (~10–15 bp), exposing the template strand
3. **Elongation** — RNA polymerase reads the template strand (3'→5') and synthesises pre-mRNA (5'→3') by adding ribonucleotides
4. **Capping & polyadenylation** — a 7-methylguanosine cap is added co-transcriptionally to the 5' end; a poly-A tail (~200 As) is added at the 3' end after cleavage of the transcript
5. **Splicing** — the spliceosome (snRNPs + proteins) removes introns and ligates exons, producing mature mRNA ready for export to the cytoplasm

questions.text: Sort the steps of **translation** (protein synthesis) in the correct order:

choices.text: The small ribosomal subunit binds to the mRNA at the 5' cap
The initiator tRNA (carrying Met) pairs with the AUG start codon
The large ribosomal subunit joins to form the complete initiation complex
Aminoacyl-tRNA enters the A site and forms a peptide bond (elongation)
The ribosome encounters a stop codon and the polypeptide is released (termination)

questions.explanation:
Translation is the process of decoding mRNA into a polypeptide:

1. **Small subunit recruitment** — the 40S ribosomal subunit binds at the 5' cap and scans the mRNA in the 5'→3' direction
2. **Start codon recognition** — the initiator Met-tRNA (carrying methionine) base-pairs with the AUG start codon in the P site
3. **Large subunit joining** — the 60S subunit docks, completing the 80S ribosome; translation is ready to begin
4. **Elongation** — each elongation cycle: (a) aminoacyl-tRNA enters the A site, (b) peptidyl transferase forms a peptide bond, (c) ribosome translocates one codon
5. **Termination** — a stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA) in the A site recruits release factors; the polypeptide is hydrolysed and released; the ribosome dissociates

questions.text: Sort the steps of **PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction)** in the correct order:

choices.text: Denaturation — the mixture is heated to ~95°C to separate the DNA strands
Annealing — the temperature drops to ~55–65°C so primers bind to the template
Extension — DNA polymerase (Taq) extends the primers at ~72°C
Repetition — the cycle is repeated 25–40 times to amplify the target sequence

questions.explanation:
Each PCR cycle consists of three temperature-controlled steps:

1. **Denaturation (~95°C)** — high heat breaks the hydrogen bonds between complementary bases, separating the double-stranded DNA into two single-stranded templates
2. **Annealing (~55–65°C)** — the temperature is lowered to allow short synthetic oligonucleotides (primers) to hybridise to their complementary sequences flanking the target region
3. **Extension (~72°C)** — Taq polymerase (a heat-stable DNA polymerase from *Thermus aquaticus*) synthesises new DNA strands from the primers in the 5'→3' direction
4. **Repetition** — the three steps are cycled 25–40 times; each cycle doubles the number of target copies, producing up to ~10⁹ copies from a single molecule

After PCR, the amplified product can be visualised on a gel, sequenced, or used for cloning.

questions.text: Sort the following events in the order they occur during **mRNA processing in eukaryotes**:

choices.text: Addition of the 7-methylguanosine (m7G) cap at the 5' end
Polyadenylation — addition of a poly-A tail at the 3' end
Splicing — removal of introns by the spliceosome
Export of the mature mRNA from the nucleus to the cytoplasm

questions.explanation:
Pre-mRNA processing in eukaryotes converts the primary transcript into a mature, export-ready mRNA:

1. **5' capping** — occurs co-transcriptionally, very soon after transcription starts; a 7-methylguanosine (m7G) cap is added to the 5' end; it protects mRNA from degradation, aids nuclear export, and is required for ribosome binding
2. **3' polyadenylation** — after the transcript is cleaved ~10–30 nt downstream of the AAUAAA signal, poly(A) polymerase adds ~200 adenine residues; this occurs before splicing is complete
3. **Splicing** — the spliceosome assembles at splice sites and removes introns through two transesterification reactions; the exons are joined to form the continuous coding sequence
4. **Nuclear export** — the mature mRNA (with cap, poly-A tail, and exons only) is exported through nuclear pore complexes into the cytoplasm, where translation occurs

Note: capping and polyadenylation can occur while the pre-mRNA is still being synthesised (co-transcriptionally).

questions.text: Sort the steps of the **signal transduction pathway (GPCR example)** in the correct order:

choices.text: A ligand (hormone or neurotransmitter) binds to the GPCR on the cell surface
The receptor changes conformation and activates the bound G-protein (GDP → GTP)
The G-protein activates adenylyl cyclase, producing cAMP from ATP
cAMP activates protein kinase A (PKA)
PKA phosphorylates target proteins, producing a cellular response

questions.explanation:
G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) signalling is a major pathway for cells to respond to extracellular signals:

1. **Ligand binding** — a hormone, neurotransmitter, or other signalling molecule binds to the extracellular domain of the GPCR
2. **G-protein activation** — the receptor changes conformation and acts as a guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF): the Gα subunit exchanges GDP for GTP and dissociates from Gβγ
3. **Second messenger production** — the activated Gα-GTP subunit stimulates adenylyl cyclase to convert ATP into cyclic AMP (cAMP), amplifying the signal
4. **PKA activation** — cAMP binds to the regulatory subunits of protein kinase A (PKA), releasing and activating the catalytic subunits
5. **Cellular response** — activated PKA phosphorylates serine/threonine residues on target proteins (enzymes, transcription factors, ion channels), producing the final physiological response

The signal is terminated by phosphodiesterase (degrades cAMP) and GTPase activity (Gα hydrolyses GTP to GDP).

questions.text: Sort the levels of **protein structure** from simplest to most complex:

choices.text: Primary structure — the linear sequence of amino acids
Secondary structure — local folding into α-helices and β-sheets
Tertiary structure — the overall 3D fold of a single polypeptide chain
Quaternary structure — assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits

questions.explanation:
Proteins are organised at four levels of structural complexity:

1. **Primary structure** — the unique sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds; determined by the gene sequence; dictates all higher levels of structure
2. **Secondary structure** — regular, local folding patterns stabilised by backbone hydrogen bonds: α-helices (coiled) and β-sheets (pleated); no side-chain interactions
3. **Tertiary structure** — the complete three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide, stabilised by hydrophobic interactions, disulfide bridges, hydrogen bonds, and ionic interactions between R-groups
4. **Quaternary structure** — applies only to proteins made of two or more polypeptide chains (subunits); the spatial arrangement of subunits; e.g., haemoglobin (2α + 2β subunits)

Protein function depends critically on its three-dimensional shape — misfolding leads to diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.

questions.text: Sort these events in the order they occur during **apoptosis (intrinsic pathway)**:

choices.text: DNA damage or cellular stress triggers release of cytochrome c from mitochondria
Cytochrome c forms the apoptosome complex with Apaf-1 and procaspase-9
Caspase-9 is activated and cleaves downstream executioner caspases (caspase-3, -6, -7)
Executioner caspases degrade cellular proteins and DNA
The cell undergoes membrane blebbing and is packaged into apoptotic bodies

questions.explanation:
The intrinsic (mitochondrial) pathway of apoptosis is activated by internal stress signals:

1. **Trigger** — DNA damage, oxidative stress, or growth factor withdrawal activates pro-apoptotic proteins (Bax, Bak) that permeabilise the outer mitochondrial membrane
2. **Cytochrome c release** — cytochrome c leaks from the mitochondrial intermembrane space into the cytoplasm; it is the key signal for apoptosome assembly
3. **Apoptosome formation** — cytochrome c binds Apaf-1, which recruits and activates procaspase-9; together they form the ~700 kDa wheel-shaped apoptosome
4. **Caspase cascade** — activated caspase-9 (initiator) cleaves and activates executioner caspases (caspase-3, -6, -7), which then cleave hundreds of cellular substrates including ICAD (releasing CAD nuclease to fragment DNA), cytoskeletal proteins, and repair enzymes
5. **Cell dismantling** — the cell shrinks, chromatin condenses, the nucleus fragments, and the plasma membrane blebs; the cell is packaged into apoptotic bodies that are recognised and phagocytosed by neighbouring cells or macrophages — no inflammation

Apoptosis is essential for development (e.g., digit separation), immune cell selection, and tumour suppression.
